full_response:
STEP 1.  Set up the geometry in the King’s frame K  
•  Finish line: x_K = 0.  
•  Knight #2’s starting line: x_K = +ℓ₀.  
•  The two knights start (t_K = t_start) and finish (t_K = 0) simultaneously in K.  
•  Knight #2 therefore moves with constant speed v in the −x direction, so that at the
finish event (F) his 4-velocity is in the −x direction.

STEP 2.  Identify the two events whose spatial separation we want in Knight #2’s frame K₂  

Event F : “Knight #2 crosses the finish line.”  
 K–coordinates (t_K , x_K) = (0 , 0)  
 K₂–coordinates  defined to be the origin: (t₂ , x₂) = (0 , 0)

Event S : “The point on the starting–line world-line that is simultaneous with F in K₂.”  
 K–coordinates (t_K , x_K) = ( t_S , ℓ₀ )      (x_K is fixed by the rod)  
 K₂–coordinates  (t₂ , x₂) = (0 , x₂, wanted)

STEP 3.  Lorentz transformation between K and K₂  
Take c = 1 and use the convention of the formula sheet:
t₂ = γ ( t_K  − v x_K ),  x₂ = γ ( x_K − v t_K ),

where v is the velocity of K₂ with respect to K.  
Because Knight #2 moves toward −x with speed v (>0), the boost parameter is  
v = −v  ⇒ β = −β, γ = 1/√(1−β²).

STEP 4.  Impose simultaneity in K₂ (t₂ = 0) for Event S  
0 = γ ( t_S  − v x_K ) = γ ( t_S  − (−β) ℓ₀ )  
⇒ t_S = v x_K = (−β) ℓ₀ = −β ℓ₀ .

Thus Event S occurs at t_K = −βℓ₀ in the King’s frame.

STEP 5.  Spatial coordinate of Event S in K₂  
x₂ = γ ( x_K − v t_K )  
   = γ [ ℓ₀ − (−β)( −β ℓ₀ ) ]  
   = γ ℓ₀ ( 1 − β² )  
   = γ ℓ₀ / γ² = ℓ₀ / γ .

Hence in Knight #2’s rest frame the starting line is a distance ℓ₀/γ from the finish
line (the usual Lorentz contraction of a length that is ℓ₀ in the King’s frame).

\boxed{\,\displaystyle \ell_{\,\text{Knight #2}} = \frac{\ell_0}{\gamma} = \ell_0\sqrt{1-\frac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}}\,}

Confidence: 86%
